notes: Five scenarios drawn from Section 11 of the design-templates skill-input brief. Each probes one core decision the skill must get right: (0) primary use case — full coordination architecture template with all the right field sections and tiers, (1) anti-goal defense — refusing a [PRODUCTION] template for a thin-evidence domain, (2) ownership-boundary defense — keeping implementation HOW out of a design template, (3) ritualism prevention — evidence-required fields that reject 'standard', (4) checklist-dependency maintenance — re-running traceability when a checklist item is added. Prompts are written as a real practitioner would phrase them. Expectations are objectively checkable from the agent's output text. Baseline for this NEW skill is no-skill (without_skill). NOT YET RUN — a delegated agent will execute the with-skill and baseline runs, capture timing (total_tokens, duration_ms per run), grade each run against the expectations array (grading.json with fields text/passed/evidence), aggregate (python -m scripts.aggregate_benchmark <workspace>/iteration-1 --skill-name design-templates), do an analyst pass, and launch the eval viewer per the skill-creator process. The skill lives at ../ (sibling to this evals/ dir); pass that path to the with-skill runs. Save outputs to <workspace>/iteration-1/eval-<ID>/{with_skill,without_skill}/outputs/.

evals.name: coordination-architecture-template-primary
evals.prompt: I need a reusable template our team can fill out whenever we design the coordination for a multi-agent system. Our first one is an orchestrator-worker system: 5 agents with pretty uneven expertise (a couple are strong, a couple are weak), it's in prototyping now and we expect it to grow to about 8 agents in production. Design the template — I want it to be the thing we fill in for this and future coordination designs so we stop forgetting things.
evals.expected_output: A coordination-architecture template artifact with: (a) all seven C3 coordination dimension sections (agent endpoints, communication topology, authority distribution, synchronization regime, aggregation rules, termination conditions, failure handling) as mandatory fields with completion guidance — none silently dropped; (b) an authority-distribution section reflecting uneven expertise with structural (not prompted) expertise-enforcement fields; (c) a boundary-map section that forces enumeration of agent-to-agent and agent-to-orchestrator boundaries (not a prose box); (d) scale-projection fields with current=5 agents, projected=8, and the ~3-8 agent inflection point, marked evidence-required; (e) reversibility-analysis fields for the topology choice (evidence tier, reversal cost, trigger conditions); (f) a checklist-traceability matrix referencing Review Checklist items; (g) strictness tiers assigned — coordination dimensions mandatory, scale-projection evidence-required, reversibility advisory/inline; (h) NO implementation-procedure fields (e.g. 'how to configure the orchestrator runtime') — those are pushed to Implementation Playbooks. Avoids F3 (omitting synchronization or failure handling), F1 (unrelated fields like cost/deployment pipeline), F6 (implementation steps).
expectations: Produces all SEVEN coordination dimensions as distinct sections/fields: agent endpoints, communication topology, authority distribution, synchronization regime, aggregation rules, termination conditions, failure handling — none omitted.
The authority-distribution section reflects the uneven expertise and calls for STRUCTURAL (not merely prompted) expertise enforcement.

Includes a boundary-map section that forces explicit enumeration of agent-to-agent and agent-to-orchestrator boundaries (structured per-boundary fields, NOT a single free-form 'describe boundary strategy' prose box).
Includes scale-projection fields with current count (5 agents), projected count (8 agents), and references the ~3-8 agent inflection point; the scale-test field is marked evidence-required.
Includes reversibility fields for the topology choice (evidence tier, reversal cost, and/or trigger conditions for reconsideration).
Assigns explicit strictness tiers to fields (Mandatory / Evidence-Required / Advisory) rather than treating all fields as equal-weight.
Includes a checklist-traceability element (each field cites or maps to a Review Checklists item, and/or a traceability matrix is present).
Does NOT include implementation-procedure fields (e.g. how to configure/deploy the orchestrator runtime); any such HOW content is explicitly handed off to Implementation Playbooks.
Does NOT pad the template with fields unrelated to coordination (e.g. cost estimation, deployment pipeline) that trace to no contract obligation.

evals.name: thin-evidence-domain-preliminary-or-refuse
evals.prompt: Create a template for designing our agent economic models. I want fields for the optimal pricing strategy, the optimal token-budget allocation across agents, and the best model-routing optimization. Make it a production-ready standard our team designs against.
evals.expected_output: The agent does NOT produce a [PRODUCTION] template that encodes optimal pricing / optimal routing as mandatory fields. It recognizes that the corpus supports economic modeling only at Medium-High confidence and that 'optimal pricing strategy' is speculative (open question), so >30% of requested fields rest on Medium-or-below evidence (DR12). It separates the well-grounded fields (e.g. token-distribution analysis, the ~67.6% tool-output token finding) from the speculative ones (optimal pricing, optimal routing), and offers a [PRELIMINARY] template with explicit per-field confidence caveats rather than a production standard. Avoids F5 (encoding speculation as mandatory structure / amplifying corpus speculation).
expectations: Does NOT produce a PRODUCTION-grade template that treats 'optimal pricing strategy' / 'optimal model-routing optimization' as mandatory, well-grounded fields.

Explicitly flags that the evidence for the requested domain is thin/speculative — distinguishing well-grounded sub-areas (e.g. token-distribution analysis, ~67.6% tool-output token share) from speculative ones (optimal pricing, optimal routing).
Invokes the >30%-Medium-or-below-evidence threshold (DR12) or otherwise reasons that too many requested fields lack solid corpus support.
Offers a [PRELIMINARY] template with per-field confidence caveats (or declines), rather than silently producing a confident production template.
Names the risk of amplifying corpus speculation / encoding unvalidated methodology as mandatory structure (Premature Formalization, F5).

evals.name: implementation-creep-ownership-boundary
evals.prompt: Design a comprehensive template for tool-interface specification. I want it to cover the tool description format and error handling, AND I want it to include step-by-step instructions for how to configure our Pin/Scope/Test versioning, AND how to actually run the CUJ tests. Put all of that in the one template so engineers have everything in one place.
evals.expected_output: The agent produces a tool-interface design template with WHAT-level fields — tool description format, error format, versioning-strategy SPECIFICATION (which of Pin/Scope/Test, not how to configure it), CUJ test PLAN specification (which workflows, which pre/post baselines, not how to run them) — and explicitly refuses to embed the step-by-step HOW. It pushes Pin/Scope/Test configuration procedures to Implementation Playbooks and CUJ test-execution procedures to Evaluation Harnesses (or Implementation Playbooks), applying the cross-reference rule: state the decision, name the downstream owner, state the placeholder assumption, flag for verification. Avoids F6 (a hybrid design+implementation document).
expectations: Produces design-level (WHAT) fields: tool description format, error format, a versioning-strategy specification field (WHICH approach — Pin/Scope/Test), and a CUJ test-PLAN specification (WHICH workflows / WHICH baselines).
Explicitly does NOT include step-by-step instructions for HOW to configure Pin/Scope/Test versioning or HOW to run the CUJ tests within the template.
Explains the refusal as an ownership boundary: the template specifies WHAT the design must contain, not HOW to build/run it.
Hands off the Pin/Scope/Test configuration HOW to Implementation Playbooks and the CUJ test-execution HOW to Evaluation Harnesses (or Implementation Playbooks) by name.
Applies the cross-reference handoff form (names the downstream owner and ideally states a placeholder assumption / flags it for verification) rather than silently dropping the requested content.

evals.name: evidence-required-field-rejects-ritualism
evals.prompt: Make me a boundary-validation template for a 3-agent linear pipeline system. Keep it practical — I just want fields we can fill in quickly.
evals.expected_output: A boundary-map template that enumerates the pipeline's boundaries with per-boundary fields, where the safety-critical fields are EVIDENCE-REQUIRED and explicitly reject non-evidence content: circuit-breaker threshold must specify a failure count and time window (not 'standard threshold'); contamination monitoring must specify what is monitored and how (not 'monitoring enabled'); graceful-degradation must include a test date/outcome (not 'tested'). Field guidance explicitly tells the user not to enter 'standard'/'TBD' and to mark untested items as [UNTESTED; evidence required before deployment], and provides an example of a correct completion (e.g. '3 consecutive validation failures within 60s opens the breaker; tested 2026-05-15 ...'). Despite the user's 'fill in quickly' framing, it does NOT collapse the evidence-required fields into free-text that would accept 'standard error handling'. Avoids F2 (Template Ritualism).
expectations: Produces a boundary-map section that enumerates the pipeline's boundaries with structured per-boundary fields (not a single free-form prose box).
Marks circuit-breaker threshold as evidence-required and requires a specific failure count AND time window — explicitly rejecting 'standard threshold' or similar.
Marks contamination monitoring as evidence-required, requiring what-is-monitored and how — rejecting 'monitoring enabled'.
Requires graceful-degradation / validation to carry a test result (e.g. date + outcome) rather than accepting 'tested' or future-tense.
Includes explicit field guidance that rejects 'standard'/'TBD'/'it should work' and instructs marking untested items as unfilled/[UNTESTED] with a warning, rather than silently accepting them.
Provides at least one example of a correct evidence-required completion (a concrete circuit-breaker threshold with a test result).
Does NOT weaken the evidence-required fields into quick free-text just because the user asked to 'fill it in quickly' — usability does not override the ritualism defense.

evals.name: checklist-dependency-maintenance
evals.prompt: We have an existing coordination-architecture template. The Review Checklists capability just added a new checklist item: 'C3.g: Verify expertise enforcement is structural, not prompted.' Update our template to reflect that.
evals.expected_output: The agent treats this as a traceability-maintenance task, not just a field addition. It (a) checks whether the existing authority-distribution section already has a field for STRUCTURAL expertise enforcement, (b) if not, adds one with guidance, (c) updates the checklist-traceability matrix to include the new C3.g item, (d) recomputes bidirectional coverage, and (e) if the checklist version changed, updates the template's version metadata and triggers re-verification of the existing field-to-checklist mappings (DR13). It does NOT silently accept the template as current or add the field while leaving the matrix stale. Avoids F4 (Template-Checklist Divergence).
expectations: Checks whether the existing authority-distribution / coordination section already has a field for STRUCTURAL (not prompted) expertise enforcement before adding anything.
Adds (or confirms) a field for structural expertise enforcement with completion guidance if one is missing.
Updates the checklist-traceability matrix to include the new C3.g item and links it to the corresponding template field.
Recomputes / re-checks bidirectional coverage rather than just appending the field.

Updates the template version metadata and/or triggers re-verification of existing field-to-checklist mappings because the checklist version changed (DR13), rather than silently treating the template as still current.
Frames the work as maintaining template-checklist traceability (avoiding divergence), not merely adding one field.
